title: Bill Frisell

infobox.value: William Richard Frisell   (1951-03-18) March 18, 1951 (age 75)  [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), Maryland, U.S.

(born March 18, 1951) is an American [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") guitarist.[[1]](#cite_note-Larkin-1) He first came to prominence at [ECM Records](/wiki/ECM_Records "ECM Records") in the 1980s, as both a [session player](/wiki/Session_musician "Session musician") and a leader.[[2]](#cite_note-2) He went on to work in a variety of contexts, notably as a participant in the Downtown Scene in New York City, where he formed a long working relationship with composer and saxophonist [John Zorn](/wiki/John_Zorn "John Zorn").[[3]](#cite_note-3) He was also a longtime member of veteran drummer [Paul Motian](/wiki/Paul_Motian "Paul Motian")'s groups from the early 1980s until Motian's death in 2011. Since the late 1990s, Frisell's output as a bandleader has also integrated prominent elements of [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"), [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"), [rock 'n' roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll"), and [Americana](/wiki/Americana_music "Americana music"). He holds six [Grammy](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards") nominations and one win.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

American jazz guitarist (born 1951)

Musical artist

**William Richard Frisell** (born March 18, 1951) is an American [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") guitarist.[[1]](#cite_note-Larkin-1) He first came to prominence at [ECM Records](/wiki/ECM_Records "ECM Records") in the 1980s, as both a [session player](/wiki/Session_musician "Session musician") and a leader.[[2]](#cite_note-2) He went on to work in a variety of contexts, notably as a participant in the Downtown Scene in New York City, where he formed a long working relationship with composer and saxophonist [John Zorn](/wiki/John_Zorn "John Zorn").[[3]](#cite_note-3) He was also a longtime member of veteran drummer [Paul Motian](/wiki/Paul_Motian "Paul Motian")'s groups from the early 1980s until Motian's death in 2011. Since the late 1990s, Frisell's output as a bandleader has also integrated prominent elements of [folk](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music"), [country](/wiki/Country_music "Country music"), [rock 'n' roll](/wiki/Rock_and_roll "Rock and roll"), and [Americana](/wiki/Americana_music "Americana music"). He holds six [Grammy](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards") nominations and one win.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Biography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bill_Frisell&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Biography")]

### Early life and career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bill_Frisell&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early life and career")]

Frisell was born in [Baltimore](/wiki/Baltimore "Baltimore"), Maryland, United States, but spent most of his youth in the [Denver](/wiki/Denver "Denver"), Colorado area.[[1]](#cite_note-Larkin-1) He studied [clarinet](/wiki/Clarinet "Clarinet") with [Richard Joiner](/wiki/Richard_Joiner "Richard Joiner") of the [Denver Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Denver_Symphony_Orchestra "Denver Symphony Orchestra") as a youth, but by his teens was more interested in guitar. He graduated from [Denver East High School](/wiki/East_High_School_(Denver,_Colorado) "East High School (Denver, Colorado)") and went to the [University of Northern Colorado](/wiki/University_of_Northern_Colorado "University of Northern Colorado") to study music.[[1]](#cite_note-Larkin-1) At UNC, he took a class taught by guitarist [Johnny Smith](/wiki/Johnny_Smith "Johnny Smith"); however, Frisell later reported that the class effectively became private lessons from Smith because the emphasis on [music theory](/wiki/Music_theory "Music theory") "was too much for everyone else—they didn't want to be learning [scales](/wiki/Scale_(music) "Scale (music)") and [inversions](/wiki/Inversion_(music) "Inversion (music)")."[[5]](#cite_note-5)

His original guitar teacher in the [Denver–Aurora metropolitan area](/wiki/Denver_metropolitan_area "Denver metropolitan area") was Dale Bruning, with whom Frisell released the 2000 duo album *Reunion*. After graduating from Northern Colorado, Frisell attended the [Berklee College of Music](/wiki/Berklee_College_of_Music "Berklee College of Music") in [Boston](/wiki/Boston "Boston"),[[1]](#cite_note-Larkin-1) where he studied with Jon Damian and [Jim Hall](/wiki/Jim_Hall_(musician) "Jim Hall (musician)").

### ECM Records years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bill_Frisell&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: ECM Records years")]

Frisell's major break came when guitarist [Pat Metheny](/wiki/Pat_Metheny "Pat Metheny") was unable to make a recording session and recommended Frisell to [Paul Motian](/wiki/Paul_Motian "Paul Motian"), who was recording *[Psalm](/wiki/Psalm_(Paul_Motian_album) "Psalm (Paul Motian album)")* (1982) for [ECM Records](/wiki/ECM_Records "ECM Records").[[6]](#cite_note-6) Frisell became ECM's in-house guitar player through the 1980s and worked on several albums, including notably [Jan Garbarek](/wiki/Jan_Garbarek "Jan Garbarek")'s 1982 *[Paths, Prints](/wiki/Paths,_Prints "Paths, Prints")*. Frisell's first release under his name was *[In Line](/wiki/In_Line_(album) "In Line (album)")* (1983), which featured a solo guitar as well as duets with bassist [Arild Andersen](/wiki/Arild_Andersen "Arild Andersen").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### New York City era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bill_Frisell&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: New York City era")]

In the 1980s, Frisell moved to [Hoboken, New Jersey](/wiki/Hoboken,_New_Jersey "Hoboken, New Jersey") and was active in the New York jazz scene.[[8]](#cite_note-8) He forged an early partnership with [John Zorn](/wiki/John_Zorn "John Zorn")—including as a member of avant-garde jazz band [Naked City](/wiki/Naked_City_(band) "Naked City (band)")—and performed or recorded with many others. He also played in Paul Motian's trio, along with saxophonist [Joe Lovano](/wiki/Joe_Lovano "Joe Lovano").[[9]](#cite_note-9)

Frisell organized a regular working group in the mid-1980s consisting of [Kermit Driscoll](/wiki/Kermit_Driscoll "Kermit Driscoll") on bass, [Joey Baron](/wiki/Joey_Baron "Joey Baron") on drums, and [Hank Roberts](/wiki/Hank_Roberts "Hank Roberts") on cello (later reduced to a trio when Roberts left). For studio projects, this group was regularly joined by other musicians.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Seattle years

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bill_Frisell&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Seattle years")]

In 1988, Frisell left New York City and moved to [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle"), Washington.[[11]](#cite_note-pacificnorthwest2001-11) In the early 1990s, Frisell made two of his best-reviewed albums: first, *[Have a Little Faith](/wiki/Have_a_Little_Faith_(Bill_Frisell_album) "Have a Little Faith (Bill Frisell album)")*, an ambitious survey of [Americana](/wiki/Americana_music "Americana music") of all stripes, from [Charles Ives](/wiki/Charles_Ives "Charles Ives") and [Aaron Copland](/wiki/Aaron_Copland "Aaron Copland") (the entirety of *[Billy the Kid](/wiki/Billy_the_Kid_(ballet) "Billy the Kid (ballet)")*) to [John Hiatt](/wiki/John_Hiatt "John Hiatt") (the title song), [Bob Dylan](/wiki/Bob_Dylan "Bob Dylan") ("[Just Like a Woman](/wiki/Just_Like_a_Woman "Just Like a Woman")") and [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna_(entertainer) "Madonna (entertainer)") (a lengthy, [psychedelic rock](/wiki/Psychedelic_rock "Psychedelic rock")-tinged version of "[Live to Tell](/wiki/Live_to_Tell "Live to Tell")");[[1]](#cite_note-Larkin-1) and second, *[This Land](/wiki/This_Land_(Bill_Frisell_album) "This Land (Bill Frisell album)")*, a complementary set of originals.[[12]](#cite_note-12) During this time, he performed with many musicians, including up-and-coming performers such as [Douglas September](/wiki/Douglas_September "Douglas September") on the album *10 Bulls*. He also branched out by performing soundtracks to silent films of [Buster Keaton](/wiki/Buster_Keaton "Buster Keaton") with his trio[[13]](#cite_note-13) and contributed to [Ryuichi Sakamoto](/wiki/Ryuichi_Sakamoto "Ryuichi Sakamoto")'s album *[Heartbeat](/wiki/Heartbeat_(Ryuichi_Sakamoto_album) "Heartbeat (Ryuichi Sakamoto album)")*.[[14]](#cite_note-allaboutjazz-14)

In the mid-1990s, Frisell disbanded his trio. He continued the trend marked by *[Have a Little Faith](/wiki/Have_a_Little_Faith_(Bill_Frisell_album) "Have a Little Faith (Bill Frisell album)")* by more explicitly incorporating elements of [bluegrass](/wiki/Bluegrass_music "Bluegrass music") and [country music](/wiki/Country_music "Country music") into his music. His friendship with [Gary Larson](/wiki/Gary_Larson "Gary Larson") led him to provide music for the TV version of *[The Far Side](/wiki/The_Far_Side "The Far Side")*[[15]](#cite_note-15) (released on the album *[Quartet](/wiki/Quartet_(Bill_Frisell_album) "Quartet (Bill Frisell album)")* along with music written for Keaton's *[Convict 13](/wiki/Convict_13 "Convict 13")*). In 1997, Frisell released the album *[Nashville](/wiki/Nashville_(Bill_Frisell_album) "Nashville (Bill Frisell album)")* on [Nonesuch Records](/wiki/Nonesuch_Records "Nonesuch Records"), which featured [Jerry Douglas](/wiki/Jerry_Douglas "Jerry Douglas"), [Viktor Krauss](/wiki/Viktor_Krauss "Viktor Krauss"), and members of [Alison Krauss](/wiki/Alison_Krauss "Alison Krauss")'s band, [Union Station](/wiki/Alison_Krauss_%26_Union_Station "Alison Krauss & Union Station").[[16]](#cite_note-nashvillescene-16) In 2000, Frisell moved to [Bainbridge Island, Washington](/wiki/Bainbridge_Island,_Washington "Bainbridge Island, Washington"), near Seattle.[[11]](#cite_note-pacificnorthwest2001-11)

### 2000 to present

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bill_Frisell&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2000 to present")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Bill_wiki_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Bill_wiki_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bill_wiki_(cropped).jpg)

Frisell with the B3 Trio at Jazz Alley, Seattle in 2004

Several of Frisell's songs, including his recording of "[Over the Rainbow](/wiki/Over_the_Rainbow "Over the Rainbow")" and "Coffaro's Theme", originally composed in 1995 for an Italian movie, *[La scuola](/wiki/La_scuola "La scuola")*, were featured in the film *[Finding Forrester](/wiki/Finding_Forrester "Finding Forrester")* in 2000.

In 1999, Frisell was commissioned by the [Walker Art Center](/wiki/Walker_Art_Center "Walker Art Center") in [Minneapolis](/wiki/Minneapolis "Minneapolis"), Minnesota, to compose *[Blues Dream](/wiki/Blues_Dream "Blues Dream")*, which he premiered on November 15, 1999. He later recorded the work for a 2001 release on Nonesuch. Also in 1999, he released *[The Sweetest Punch](/wiki/The_Sweetest_Punch "The Sweetest Punch")*, which featured a seven-piece jazz ensemble reworking the tunes written and recorded by [Elvis Costello](/wiki/Elvis_Costello "Elvis Costello") and [Burt Bacharach](/wiki/Burt_Bacharach "Burt Bacharach") on *[Painted from Memory](/wiki/Painted_from_Memory "Painted from Memory")*.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Between 2003 and 2005, Frisell acted as musical director for *Century of Song*, a series of concerts at the German [Ruhrtriennale](/wiki/Ruhrtriennale "Ruhrtriennale") arts festival (produced by [Lee Townsend](/wiki/Lee_Townsend "Lee Townsend")). Frisell invited artists including [Rickie Lee Jones](/wiki/Rickie_Lee_Jones "Rickie Lee Jones"), [Elvis Costello](/wiki/Elvis_Costello "Elvis Costello"), [Petra Haden](/wiki/Petra_Haden "Petra Haden"), [Jenny Scheinman](/wiki/Jenny_Scheinman "Jenny Scheinman"), [Suzanne Vega](/wiki/Suzanne_Vega "Suzanne Vega"), [Arto Lindsay](/wiki/Arto_Lindsay "Arto Lindsay"), [Loudon Wainwright III](/wiki/Loudon_Wainwright_III "Loudon Wainwright III"), [Vic Chesnutt](/wiki/Vic_Chesnutt "Vic Chesnutt"), [Van Dyke Parks](/wiki/Van_Dyke_Parks "Van Dyke Parks"), [Buddy Miller](/wiki/Buddy_Miller "Buddy Miller"), [Ron Sexsmith](/wiki/Ron_Sexsmith "Ron Sexsmith"), and [Chip Taylor](/wiki/Chip_Taylor "Chip Taylor") to perform their favorite songs in new arrangements.[[16]](#cite_note-nashvillescene-16)

In 2003, Frisell's *[The Intercontinentals](/wiki/The_Intercontinentals "The Intercontinentals")* was nominated for a [Grammy Award](/wiki/Grammy_Awards "Grammy Awards"); he won the 2005 [Grammy Award for Best Contemporary Jazz Album](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Contemporary_Jazz_Album "Grammy Award for Best Contemporary Jazz Album") for his album *[Unspeakable](/wiki/Unspeakable_(album) "Unspeakable (album)")*. His 2008 album *[History, Mystery](/wiki/History,_Mystery "History, Mystery")* was nominated for a 2009 Grammy award for [Best Jazz Instrumental Album, Individual or Group](/wiki/Grammy_Award_for_Best_Jazz_Instrumental_Album "Grammy Award for Best Jazz Instrumental Album").[[18]](#cite_note-18) Frisell was also a judge for the sixth annual Independent Music Awards to support independent artists careers.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

In 2008, Frisell performed as a featured guest on [Earth](/wiki/Earth_(American_band) "Earth (American band)")'s album *[The Bees Made Honey in the Lion's Skull](/wiki/The_Bees_Made_Honey_in_the_Lion%27s_Skull "The Bees Made Honey in the Lion's Skull")*. The following year, Frisell was featured in a duet rendition of [Leonard Cohen](/wiki/Leonard_Cohen "Leonard Cohen")'s "[Hallelujah](/wiki/Hallelujah_(Leonard_Cohen_song) "Hallelujah (Leonard Cohen song)")" with singer-songwriter [Sam Shrieve](/w/index.php?title=Sam_Shrieve&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sam Shrieve (page does not exist)")—the recording was released on Shrieve's debut album, *Bittersweet Lullabies*.

In 2010, Frisell started working with the [Savoy Jazz](/wiki/Savoy_Records "Savoy Records") label and released *[Beautiful Dreamers](/wiki/Beautiful_Dreamers_(album) "Beautiful Dreamers (album)")* in August 2010, then a second release of *[Sign of Life](/wiki/Sign_of_Life:_Music_for_858_Quartet "Sign of Life: Music for 858 Quartet")* in April 2011. Also, on January 25, 2011, Frisell and [Vinicius Cantuária](/wiki/Vinicius_Cantu%C3%A1ria "Vinicius Cantuária") released *[Lágrimas Mexicanas](/wiki/L%C3%A1grimas_Mexicanas "Lágrimas Mexicanas")* on the [E1](/wiki/Entertainment_One_Music "Entertainment One Music") label.[[14]](#cite_note-allaboutjazz-14)

In June 2011, Frisell, [Lee Townsend](/wiki/Lee_Townsend "Lee Townsend"), and their frequent collaborator, Cantuária, participated in [TEDx](/wiki/TED_(conference) "TED (conference)") GoldenGateED's program "Teaching Compassion" in Oakland, California. Frisell and Cantuária performed separately, and Townsend assisted with the technical aspects of the event.[[20]](#cite_note-20) In September 2011, Frisell released *[All We Are Saying](/wiki/All_We_Are_Saying "All We Are Saying")*, a full-length offering of his interpretations of [John Lennon](/wiki/John_Lennon "John Lennon")'s music. Frisell's quintet includes violinist [Jenny Scheinman](/wiki/Jenny_Scheinman "Jenny Scheinman"), pedal steel and acoustic guitarist [Greg Leisz](/wiki/Greg_Leisz "Greg Leisz"), bassist [Tony Scherr](/wiki/Tony_Scherr "Tony Scherr"), and drummer [Kenny Wollesen](/wiki/Kenny_Wollesen "Kenny Wollesen"). In 2017, Frisell received an honorary doctorate of music from his alma mater, the Berklee College of Music.[[21]](#cite_note-21) Frisell has lived in [Brooklyn](/wiki/Brooklyn "Brooklyn") since 2017.[[22]](#cite_note-newyorker-22)

In 2021, a video was recorded at the [Village Vanguard](/wiki/Village_Vanguard "Village Vanguard") in New York and was published by [Blue Note Records](/wiki/Blue_Note_Records "Blue Note Records") on [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube").[[23]](#cite_note-23)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

*Bill Frisell, Beautiful Dreamer: The Guitarist Who Changed the Sound of American Music*, a biography on Frisell written by Philip Watson, was published by [Faber & Faber](/wiki/Faber_%26_Faber "Faber & Faber") on May 24, 2022.[[22]](#cite_note-newyorker-22)
